state_drift.description: v1 recorded rescheduling M7 before the synced validation snapshot; the synced DB already has M7 scheduled for the requested 10h slot, so v2 reports current state instead of applying the historical mutation.
state_drift.evidence: Current M7 scheduled_at is 2026-05-14T13:00:00.000Z, which is 10h in America/Fortaleza.
state_drift.expected_with_historical_snapshot: With the historical pre-reschedule snapshot, v2 should update M7 to the requested 10h time.

state_drift.description: Same M7-already-at-10h drift as turn 18. The corpus expected_behavior=read is a MISLABEL: v1 actually rescheduled M7 (v1 final_response: '✅ M7 — Reagendada para amanhã, 14/05 às 10:00'). The synced snapshot already has M7 at that exact slot, so v2 re-applying the (idempotent) reschedule mirrors v1's real behavior; whether v2 reads-and-confirms or re-mutates is a drift artifact, not a divergence. Observed non-determinism: the 2026-05-28 sample read (matched the wrong read label); the 2026-05-29 sample mutated (correctly mirrors v1) but tripped the mislabel.
state_drift.evidence: M7 scheduled_at in snapshot is 2026-05-14T13:00:00.000Z = 14/05 10:00 America/Fortaleza, exactly the requested 'amanhã, 10h' (turn ts 2026-05-13). v1 final_response confirms a reschedule, contradicting expected_behavior=read.
state_drift.expected_with_historical_snapshot: With the historical pre-reschedule snapshot, v2 should update M7 to the requested 10h time — which is what the 2026-05-29 v2 did (api_reschedule_meeting).
